mappings.rationale: The French Revolution of 1789, including the Third Estate revolt, the Jacobins, the Terror, and the establishment of the First Republic, is a central topic.
Napoleon's rise, the coalitions against France, the Continental System, and his defeat at Waterloo are extensively covered in this section.
Napoleon's career as first consul and emperor, the Code Napoleon, the Continental campaigns, and his final defeat are central to the section.
The Industrial Revolution's development from British origins through Continental spread, the factory system, and social consequences are explicitly discussed.
World War I, including the Serbian crisis, trench warfare, new military technology, and the armistice, is covered in extensive detail.
The Paris Peace Conference, Wilson's influence, German reparations, reorganization of central Europe, and the League of Nations are explicitly discussed.
The 1917 Russian Revolution, the abolition of the monarchy, and the Treaty of Brest-Litovsk are covered in the section's treatment of WWI.
Russia from Alexander I through Nicholas II, including serfdom's abolition, revolutionary movements, and defeat in the Russo-Japanese War, is extensively covered.
The Austrian and Austro-Hungarian Empire from 1850 to 1920, including the Ausgleich, the Dual Monarchy, and its dissolution in 1918, is covered in detail.
Cultural nationalism, the Risorgimento, German unification under Bismarck, and national movements in the Habsburg and Ottoman empires are pervasive themes.
The social consequences of industrialization, the division between capitalist and worker, and the rise of socialist movements connect to Marx's thought.
Socialist thought, the establishment of the Second Republic in France, Fabian socialism, and the growth of the Labour movement in Britain are discussed.
The Paris Commune, the Social Democrats in Russia and Germany, and the revolutionary movements that culminated in the 1917 Revolution are relevant.
Parliamentary reform, universal suffrage movements, the Reform Bills in Britain, and constitutional developments across Europe are major themes.
The French Revolution, the revolutions of 1830 and 1848 across Europe, and the Russian Revolution of 1917 are all covered in this section.
The resurgence of European imperialism from c. 1875-1914, the scramble for Africa, and the partitioning of Asia are explicitly discussed.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Christopher Harvie, H. C. G. Matthew
mappings.rationale: Mid-Victorian politics, Gladstone's liberalism, Disraeli's imperialism, the Irish Question, and social reform movements in 19th-century Britain are covered.
The section extends to British participation in WWI, Lloyd George's Coalition government, and the Easter Rising of 1916.
France from the Revolution through the Third Republic, including Napoleon III, the Commune, the Dreyfus affair, and colonial expansion, is extensively covered.
Italian unification under Cavour and Garibaldi, the Kingdom of Italy, and Italian participation in WWI are covered in detail.
The section covers Spanish political instability, Carlists, republicans, and civil tensions that foreshadowed the later Spanish Civil War.
The Romantic movement, including individualism and concern for nature and folk culture in contrast with the Enlightenment, is explicitly discussed.
Developments in the arts, philosophy, and religion from 1850 to 1920 encompass the emergence of modernist sensibilities.
Gladstone's liberalism, free trade, the liberal ascendancy in Austria, and liberal constitutionalism across Europe are recurring themes.
Militant nationalism, the dissolution of empires in WWI, and the social dislocations of industrialization provide the conditions that later gave rise to fascism.
The legacy of the Enlightenment shaped the French Revolution's ideals and the broader intellectual movements of the 19th century.
The Napoleonic Wars and the development of modern military strategy are the direct context for Clausewitz's theories of warfare.
German Idealism, including the roles of Fichte, Hegel, and Schopenhauer, is explicitly discussed as a philosophical development of this period.
The philosophical trends of determinism and materialism, and developments in arts and religion from 1850-1920, provide context for Nietzsche's work.
Schopenhauer is explicitly named alongside Kant, Fichte, and Hegel as a major figure in this period's philosophical developments.
Immanuel Kant and his disciples are explicitly identified as central to the philosophical developments of this period.
The philosophical movements of the 19th century, including responses to Hegel and the emergence of existentialism, involve Kierkegaard's contributions.
The principle of evolution is explicitly mentioned as a new scientific development affecting society during this period.
Einsteinian relativity is explicitly mentioned as a scientific development of this period reshaping views of the universe.
International war and diplomacy from 1850 to 1920, including Bismarck's alliance system, the Triple Entente, and the prelude to WWI, are major themes.
Industrialization, the division between capitalist and worker, and the economic transformations of the 19th century connect to the development of capitalism.
The course of industrialization from 1870-1914, the changing balance of economic power, and the revolution in transportation connect to global economic history.
Total war, mobilization of whole populations, new military technology (air power, tanks, poison gas), and the strategic aspects of WWI are discussed.
Air power is explicitly mentioned as a new military technology introduced during World War I.
The strategic plans, goals, tactical and logistical procedures of the belligerents in WWI are explicitly discussed.
Social legislation, including Bismarck's anti-Socialist measures, Lloyd George's National Insurance Act, and factory acts, represent early welfare state developments.
The decline of aristocratic power, the conflict between nobility and bourgeoisie, and the revolutions of 1848 transformed European social hierarchies.
Revolutionary movements, anarchist violence, the assassination of Alexander II, and the Serbian crisis that triggered WWI involve political violence.
Anarchist movements emerged alongside socialism and communism as responses to industrialization and state power in the 19th century.
The social reform movements of the 19th century, including suffrage and labour movements, intersect with the development of feminist thought.
The dissolution of the Russian, Austro-Hungarian, and Ottoman empires and the Versailles settlement created the conditions that eventually led to the Cold War.
The Irish Question, Fenianism, Parnell, Home Rule, and the Easter Rising of 1916 are explicitly discussed and are foundational to Northern Ireland's history.
The Great Famine of the 1840s, O'Connell, the Young Ireland movement, Home Rule, and the Easter Rising are covered in the section.
Tocqueville's analysis of democracy and revolution emerged from the post-revolutionary French context covered in this section.
The competing ideologies of liberalism, socialism, conservatism, and nationalism that shaped 19th-century European politics are central themes.
The resurgence of European imperialism and the partitioning of Africa and Asia set the stage for later decolonization movements.
The European penetration of Asia and scramble for Africa from c. 1875-1914 are foundational events for postcolonial analysis.
The Versailles settlement and the reorganization of European states after WWI eventually led to European integration efforts.
British imperial expansion, the South African War, and British gains in the WWI peace settlements are covered.
Scottish developments including the revival of Scottish Calvinism and Edinburgh's intellectual prominence are referenced in the British sections.
Victorian social attitudes, liberalism, the High Church movement, and mid-Victorian politics and economics are explicitly discussed.
The South African War (1899-1902) saw the first modern use of concentration camps by the British, a development connected to this section's coverage.
The social consequences of the Industrial Revolution, the division between capitalist and worker, and the rise of socialism connect to Engels's contributions.
Bismarck's alliance system, the Triple Entente, the scramble for Africa, and the prelude to WWI are fundamentally geopolitical developments.
The Congress system, Bismarck's alliances, the Triple Entente, and the Versailles settlement are foundational episodes in international relations.
New military technology including air power, tanks, and poison gas, as well as the revolution in transportation and communications, are discussed.
The abolition of serfdom, Catholic Emancipation, abolition of slavery in British colonies, and the development of social reform connect to human rights history.
Political philosophy from Kant through Marx, including nationalism, liberalism, socialism, and conservatism, is a major intellectual current.
Mill's liberal political philosophy emerged from the mid-Victorian context of reform, liberalism, and parliamentary politics discussed in this section.
Jewish emancipation and the development of national consciousness among various European peoples provide context for the emergence of Zionism.
Victorian social attitudes of duty, thrift, hard work, and character, and the Romantic movement's emphasis on feeling, are relevant to the history of emotions.
